claims.claim: Preventive detention laws total 7 across Malaysian statutes
claims.source: Malaysia Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, Act 747, official Laws of Malaysia PDF via AGC, gazetted 22 June 2012, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; Dangerous Drugs (Special Preventive Measures) Act 1985, Act 316, official Laws of Malaysia reprint, https://www.aadk.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/Act-316-Dangerous-Drugs-Special-Preventive-Measures-Act-1985.pdf; Prevention of Crime Act 1959, Act 297, Ministry of Home Affairs PDF, https://www.moha.gov.my/utama/images/Bahagian%20Keselamatan%20dan%20Ketenteraman%20Awam/AKTA/POCA/Prevention_of_Crime_Act.pdf; Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim v Kerajaan Malaysia & Anor [2021], court ruling summary discussing POCA and POTA detention powers, https://www.elaw.my/JE/01/JE_2021_38.html; Malaysian Bar press release, 1 September 2018, noting the Emergency Ordinance was repealed, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/press_statements/press_release_the_malaysian_bar_supports_the_attorney_general_in_rejecting_draconian_preventive_detention_laws.html?date=2018-09-01
claims.correction: Do not state 7 current preventive detention laws unless a current official legal list supports that count. The Emergency Ordinance is not a current statute. SOSMA provides up to 28 days investigative detention for security offences, which is materially different from POCA/POTA/DDA detention-order regimes. Immigration detention and NSC Act powers should not be grouped as preventive detention without a precise legal basis.
claims.stale_check: No evidence this was true as of 2025; the Emergency Ordinance had already ceased/repealed long before 2025.

claims.claim: Malaysia maintains 7 separate laws allowing preventive detention without trial, more than any other ASEAN nation as of 2025.
claims.source: No primary comparative ASEAN source found. Malaysia sources checked include Act 747 SOSMA, Act 316 DDA, Act 297 POCA, and court/legal references to POTA; no primary source verified a 7-law count or an ASEAN ranking.
claims.correction: Split into two claims and source each: one on Malaysia's current laws, one on comparative ASEAN law. The ASEAN superlative should be removed unless supported by a comprehensive official or peer-reviewed comparative legal source.
claims.stale_check: No recent change found that would make the 7-law ASEAN ranking verifiable.

claims.claim: 7 separate Malaysian laws allow detention without trial as of 2025
claims.source: Malaysian Bar, 1 September 2018, states the Emergency Ordinance was repealed; https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/press_statements/press_release_the_malaysian_bar_supports_the_attorney_general_in_rejecting_draconian_preventive_detention_laws.html?date=2018-09-01. Act 747 SOSMA official PDF, section 4 provides 24 hours plus up to 28 days for investigation, not a detention-order regime; https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf
claims.correction: Use a narrower statement: Malaysia retains several detention-without-trial or administrative detention mechanisms, including POCA, POTA, DDA, and SOSMA's extended investigative detention, but the exact count needs a current official legal audit.
claims.stale_check: Not stale; EO had been repealed/lapsed before 2025.

claims.claim: SOSMA, POCA, DDA, POTA, NSC Act, Immigration Act, and EO all permit preventive detention
claims.source: SOSMA Act 747 official PDF, section 4, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; DDA Act 316 official PDF, sections arrangement and preamble, https://www.aadk.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/Act-316-Dangerous-Drugs-Special-Preventive-Measures-Act-1985.pdf; Immigration Act 1959/63, Immigration Department PDF, section 34 removal detention, https://www.imi.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/IMMIGRATION-ACT-1959-63-PDF.pdf; National Security Council Act 2016, official NSC PDF, https://www.mkn.gov.my/web/wp-content/uploads/sites/3/2019/08/Akta-MKN-2016-BI.pdf; Malaysian Bar on EO repeal, 1 September 2018, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/press_statements/press_release_the_malaysian_bar_supports_the_attorney_general_in_rejecting_draconian_preventive_detention_laws.html?date=2018-09-01
claims.correction: EO should be removed because it is not current. Immigration Act section 34 is detention pending removal, not ordinary preventive detention. NSC Act should not be included without citing a specific section authorising preventive detention. SOSMA should be described as extended investigative detention under security offences, not as the same kind of preventive detention as POCA/POTA/DDA.
claims.stale_check: No indication that EO became current again by 2025.

claims.claim: Article 149 of Federal Constitution specifically enables preventive detention laws
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Article 149, WIPO Lex consolidated text, lines 2058-2070, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823; Dangerous Drugs (Special Preventive Measures) Act 1985 preamble cites Article 149, https://www.aadk.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/Act-316-Dangerous-Drugs-Special-Preventive-Measures-Act-1985.pdf
claims.correction: Article 149 validates special laws against subversion, organised violence and acts prejudicial to public order/security even if inconsistent with Articles 5, 9, 10 or 13. It can support preventive detention statutes, but the text is broader and does not simply or exclusively 'enable preventive detention laws'.

claims.claim: Constitutional provision drafted during Emergency period remains unchanged
claims.source: Parliament repository, Constitution (Amendment) Bill/Act materials 1960, notes the constitutional position on preventive detention under Articles 149 or 150, https://repositori.parlimen.gov.my/bitstream/123456789/1607/12/D.R.0015-1960.pdf; Federal Constitution WIPO Lex amendment notes and current text, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
claims.correction: Remove 'remains unchanged'. Article 149 has a constitutional history involving amendments after Merdeka. If making a historical claim, specify the exact original text, amendment Act, and date.
claims.stale_check: No recent change; the problem is historical/legal accuracy.

claims.claim: UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention reviewed 14 Malaysian cases since 2018
claims.source: UN Digital Library/OHCHR opinions found include Opinion No. 37/2018 concerning a minor in Malaysia, https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/3932749/files/A_HRC_WGAD_2018_37-EN.pdf; Opinion No. 90/2018 concerning Malaysia, https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/3932992/files/A_HRC_WGAD_2018_90-EN.pdf; Opinion No. 51/2020 concerning Arif Komiş, Ülkü Komiş and four minors (Malaysia and Turkey), https://www.ohchr.org/sites/default/files/Documents/Issues/Detention/Opinions/Session88/A_HRC_WGAD_2020_51_Advance_Edited_Version.pdf; Opinion No. 8/2022 concerning Alettin Duman and Tamer Tibik (Malaysia and Turkey), https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/3993275
claims.correction: I could verify several Malaysia-related WGAD opinions since 2018, but not a total of 14 'Malaysian cases'. Clarify whether 'cases' means opinions, individuals, communications, or Malaysia-related incidents, then cite the WGAD database or each opinion.
claims.stale_check: No evidence of a recent update making the 14-case count verifiable within the checked sources.

claims.claim: Group found arbitrary detention in 11 of 14 cases examined
claims.source: OHCHR Opinion No. 51/2020 states the Working Group considered the family case arbitrary under categories I, II, III and V; https://www.ohchr.org/sites/default/files/Documents/Issues/Detention/Opinions/Session88/A_HRC_WGAD_2020_51_Advance_Edited_Version.pdf. UN Digital Library Opinion No. 8/2022 concerns Malaysia and Turkey; https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/3993275. No primary source verified an 11-of-14 aggregate.
claims.correction: Do not use the 11-of-14 statistic unless a primary WGAD dataset or a transparent count of individual WGAD opinions supports it.

claims.claim: Combined total of approximately 2,800 people held under preventive detention in 2025
claims.source: No Malaysian primary source found for a 2,800 preventive-detention total in 2025. Searches for the exact figure returned non-Malaysia results, including Kashmir-related reporting and UN communications, not Malaysian detention totals.
claims.correction: Remove the 2,800 figure unless sourced to Malaysian parliamentary answers, Home Ministry statistics, prison/immigration data, or an official response. If combining categories, state exactly which statutes and whether the number means arrests, current detainees, remand, detention orders, restriction orders, or immigration detention.
claims.stale_check: No evidence this became correct by May 2026; the exact figure appears likely misattributed or unsourced.

claims.claim: Families and communities bear hidden costs of lost breadwinners and social stigma
claims.source: No primary source in the analysis or search results quantified or directly documented this specific claim for the 2025 detainee population. Human Rights Watch documented harms from immigration detention through interviews in 2023, but that is not a primary source for this exact combined preventive-detention claim: https://www.hrw.org/report/2024/03/05/we-cant-see-sun/malaysias-arbitrary-detention-migrants-and-refugees
claims.correction: Frame as a supported qualitative claim only if backed by detainee-family testimony, court affidavits, official inquiry findings, or peer-reviewed research; otherwise remove or soften.

claims.claim: 7 laws enabling detention without trial is a system designed for control, not security
claims.source: Primary legal sources verify some detention powers but not the asserted motive or system-wide purpose: Federal Constitution Article 149, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823; SOSMA Act 747, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; DDA Act 316, https://www.aadk.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/Act-316-Dangerous-Drugs-Special-Preventive-Measures-Act-1985.pdf
claims.correction: This is a normative interpretation, not a fact. It should be clearly presented as analysis or opinion, and it should not rest on the unverified 7-law count.

claims.claim: Suaram documented all 7 preventive detention regimes in 2024 annual report
claims.source: SUARAM launch page for Malaysia Human Rights Report Overview 2024 discusses SOSMA, POCA amendments and DD(SPM)A arrests but does not verify an all-7-regime list in the accessible text, 29 November 2024, https://www.suaram.net/post/launch-of-suaram-malaysia-human-rights-report-overview-2024; SUARAM Right to Trial page lists SOSMA, POCA, POTA and DDA while discussing historical ISA/EO, https://www.suaram.net/right-to-trial
claims.correction: Cite the exact SUARAM report page or table if it lists seven regimes. Based on accessible SUARAM material, the claim should be narrowed to SUARAM documenting continued use of SOSMA and DD(SPM)A and discussing POCA/POTA/DDA/SOSMA detention-without-trial laws.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Malaysia maintains 7 separate laws allowing preventive detention without trial, more than any other ASEAN nation as of 2025.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The 7-law count is not supported by the checked primary legal sources, includes at least one repealed/lapsed law, and the ASEAN superlative was not verified by any primary comparative source.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Malaysian Bar on EO repeal, 1 September 2018, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/press_statements/press_release_the_malaysian_bar_supports_the_attorney_general_in_rejecting_draconian_preventive_detention_laws.html?date=2018-09-01; Act 747 SOSMA, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: SOSMA, POCA, DDA, POTA, NSC Act, Immigration Act, and EO all permit preventive detention
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The list collapses different legal mechanisms into one category: SOSMA investigative detention, immigration detention pending removal, security-area powers under NSC Act, and repealed EO are not equivalent to current POCA/POTA/DDA detention-order regimes.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: SOSMA Act 747 section 4, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf; Immigration Act 1959/63 section 34, https://www.imi.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/IMMIGRATION-ACT-1959-63-PDF.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: UN Working Group on Arbitrary Detention reviewed 14 Malaysian cases since 2018
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Several Malaysia-related WGAD opinions were found, but the exact count of 14 cases was not verified from a primary WGAD source, and the term 'cases' is ambiguous.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: WGAD opinion search database, https://wgad-opinions.ohchr.org/; UN Digital Library Opinion No. 37/2018, https://digitallibrary.un.org/record/3932749/files/A_HRC_WGAD_2018_37-EN.pdf

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Group found arbitrary detention in 11 of 14 cases examined
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The aggregate ratio was not traceable to a primary WGAD source or transparent count of opinions/individuals.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Combined total of approximately 2,800 people held under preventive detention in 2025
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No Malaysian primary source was found for this number; exact-number searches returned Kashmir-related 2,800 detention reports, suggesting possible misattribution.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: No Malaysian primary source found; non-Malaysia UN communication example: https://spcommreports.ohchr.org/TMResultsBase/DownLoadPublicCommunicationFile?gId=30287

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: 7 laws enabling detention without trial is a system designed for control, not security
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This states motive and system purpose as fact while relying on an unverified 7-law count. Primary legal texts show powers and statutory purposes, not proof of the asserted motive.

omitted_facts: The Emergency Ordinance is not a current detention law; the Malaysian Bar describes it as repealed, and the EO should not be counted as a 2025 statute. Source: Malaysian Bar, 1 September 2018, https://www.malaysianbar.org.my/press_statements/press_release_the_malaysian_bar_supports_the_attorney_general_in_rejecting_draconian_preventive_detention_laws.html?date=2018-09-01

SOSMA section 4 allows arrest and detention for investigation for 24 hours, extendable by a Superintendent of Police for not more than 28 days; it also says detention cannot be solely for political belief or political activity. Source: Security Offences (Special Measures) Act 2012, Act 747, https://lom.agc.gov.my/ilims/upload/portal/akta/outputaktap/20120622_747_BI_Act%20747%20BI.pdf

DDA Act 316 expressly says it is an Act to provide for preventive detention of persons associated with dangerous-drug trafficking activity, and it is enacted pursuant to Article 149. Source: Dangerous Drugs (Special Preventive Measures) Act 1985, https://www.aadk.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/Act-316-Dangerous-Drugs-Special-Preventive-Measures-Act-1985.pdf

Immigration Act section 34 allows detention of a person ordered to be removed for such period as necessary to arrange removal; that is administrative immigration detention, not automatically the same legal category as preventive detention without trial. Source: Immigration Act 1959/63, https://www.imi.gov.my/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/IMMIGRATION-ACT-1959-63-PDF.pdf

Article 149 validates special laws against listed threats notwithstanding inconsistency with Articles 5, 9, 10 or 13, but it does not itself use the simple formulation 'seven preventive detention laws'. Source: Federal Constitution Article 149, https://www.wipo.int/wipolex/en/legislation/details/6823
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only two previous T4A related issues, which are circular sources and not primary verification. It does not cite Malaysian statutes, parliamentary records, court rulings, Home Ministry statistics, WGAD opinions, SUARAM report pages, or comparative ASEAN legal sources. The accessible evidence base is therefore extremely narrow and institutionally one-sided, with major gaps in government/legal primary sources, current detainee statistics, and comparative regional data.
overall_assessment: The analysis is factually unreliable in its current form. Its central 7-law count is not verified and appears to include at least one repealed/lapsed law while grouping legally different detention mechanisms as if they were equivalent. The strongest supported point is that Malaysian law contains several detention-without-trial or extended-detention mechanisms under statutes such as POCA, POTA, DDA and SOSMA, but the exact count, 2,800 detainee figure, WGAD 11-of-14 statistic, and ASEAN superlative should not be published without fresh primary sourcing.
